details: Concrete example to keep in view: CM remains on 10.167.2.150, an LDU first uses .153, and a later rebuilt remote agent reuses .153.
Treat this as a behavioral note for later cookbook review, not as an active backlog item or a proven universal rule.
Reviewed interactively with the operator on 2026-04-04 while defining the collaborative idea-log workflow.
notes.summary: In reusable lab deployments, if the central manager stays in place while a node IP is reused for a different role, the reused IP may need explicit cleanup of the earlier node identity on the central manager before reattachment behaves cleanly.
notes.title: Central manager cleanup may be needed when reused node IP changes role

details: Frontline playbooks should start from validated technical paths such as Service State, Host Health, Logs, Data Collection and Processing, and Distributed Node Role.
The CLI dashboard is only a front-door filter for core `ms-*` service state and should not be treated as a rich diagnostic surface.
If the core services are up, the next serious playbook move should go to Logs instead of forcing a service-state or GUI-first explanation.
Deeper runtime-owner concepts like listener ownership, route ownership, or backend handoff belong in cookbook or deeper successor layers, not frontline triage pages.
The operator explicitly rejected coaching sections about when to restart, when to escalate, what to report, or what evidence to capture because experienced engineers already own those judgments.
notes.summary: Frontline ADF successor playbooks should start from validated technical paths, keep the CLI `ms-*` service dashboard as a narrow front-door filter, and move quickly into Logs for real triage depth instead of coaching experienced engineers or drifting into R&D-depth runtime analysis.
notes.title: Frontline playbooks should start from technical paths and treat service state as a front-door filter

details: The new review surfaces are docs/specs/adf-successor-starlight-review-shell-plan-v1.md, docs/specs/adf-successor-adf-dev-headless-browser-host-plan-v1.md, and docs/specs/adf-successor-site-map-and-wireframe-review-v1.md.

adf-dev already serves the older ADF review page on 10.167.2.151:18082 but still lacks Chromium and Playwright, so remote browser review should wait for host provisioning instead of assuming it is ready.
The first paired successor review pages should be Service State and Core Service Groups by Node Role before the shell widens further.

The review baseline now treats `/playbooks/` and `/cookbooks/` as real top-level routes and resolves `Service State` branch destinations to concrete `Logs` anchors or real routes instead of vague placeholders.

The review-only rewrite now resolves Playbooks and Cookbooks to real index routes at /playbooks/ and /cookbooks/, and the Service State skeleton now branches to concrete Logs anchors or Host Health instead of abstract destinations.
The first cookbook skeleton now uses explicit evidence-state sections: Validated behavior, Observed practice, Operator theory, and Not proven.

The follow-up swarm review tightened the skeletons again: top navigation now resolves to `/playbooks/` and `/cookbooks/`, `Service State` now branches to concrete `Logs` anchors or `Host Health`, and the first cookbook skeleton now uses explicit evidence-state sections plus a practical role matrix.

The current reviewed first implementation set now includes a real `Logs` page skeleton behind the `Service State` anchors, with anchor sections for single-service failure, multiple-service failure, not-responding state, and core-services-up paths.

A first bounded successor shell is now implemented locally under dist/candidates/adf-starlight-review-shell/starlight-site with six built routes: /, /playbooks/, /playbooks/service-state/, /playbooks/logs/, /cookbooks/, and /cookbooks/core-service-groups-by-node-role/.
The local implementation intentionally keeps the older ADF Starlight line untouched and uses a separate successor site root plus a separate preview port at 18083.
The current local toolchain that built successfully is Astro 6.1.3 plus @astrojs/starlight 0.38.2, after the older donor versions proved incompatible with the current local environment.

The adf-dev browser-host plan is now implemented in a bounded fallback form: sudo was password-gated, so the working runtime uses ~/packfactory-browser-review with Playwright, Playwright-managed Chromium, and user-local extracted Ubuntu libraries under ~/packfactory-browser-review/system-libs.

The host-local proof now opens the older ADF route at http://127.0.0.1:18082/playbooks/asms-ui-is-down/ and the new successor shell at http://127.0.0.1:18083/, including the successor Service State page.
notes.summary: The successor now has a concrete review-only publication baseline: reuse the older ADF Starlight publisher patterns, keep the successor shell path-first and cookbook-separated, publish Service State plus Core Service Groups by Node Role first, and treat adf-dev browser provisioning as a host-managed prerequisite before remote headless review.
notes.title: Successor review shell and adf-dev browser host should be finalized before implementation

details: The operator wants ADF support pages to exploit a real team strength: engineers read all day and learn how to spot key information quickly.
The visible shell should therefore behave like a stable reading system rather than a generic docs site or a collection of one-off page designs.
Playbooks and cookbooks should preserve fixed archetypes, fixed section order, and fixed label meaning so engineers build scan memory over time.
Navigation should stay subordinate to the main reading lane and should be dropped or reduced when it consumes valuable diagnostic screen space.
notes.summary: ADF support pages should use a daily-reader content grammar: a small number of fixed page archetypes, stable section order, stable label meaning, and quiet navigation so engineers can build scan memory and recognize key information faster over repeated use.
notes.title: ADF should use a daily-reader content grammar for support pages
